title: Wood's Conjecture on Exact Clique Count for $K_t$-Minor Free Graphs
statement_text: The maximum number of cliques a $K_t$-minor free graph on $n$ vertices can have is $2^{t-2}(n-t+3)$ if and only if $t \leq 49$.
context_text: Wood determined for $t \leq 9$ and $n \geq 4$ that the exact maximum number of cliques in a $K_t$-minor free graph on $n$ vertices is $2^{t-2}(n-t+3)$, and conjectured this formula gives the true maximum precisely for $t \leq 49$. For larger $t$, a different construction — the disjoint union of complements of perfect matchings on $2\lceil 2t/3 \rceil - 2$ vertices — achieves $3^{2t/3-o(t)}n$ cliques, eventually surpassing the conjectured bound.
paper_contribution: Determines the correct exponential constant, proving every $K_t$-minor free graph on $n$ vertices has at most $3^{2t/3+o(t)}n$ cliques (tight for $n \geq 4t/3$); since $3^{2t/3} > 2^{t-2}$ for large $t$, this confirms that Wood's exact formula fails for sufficiently large $t$, consistent with the conjectured threshold $t = 49$.
notes: PDF source — the formula $2^{t-2}(n-t+3)$ is reconstructed from garbled PDF rendering; attributed to Wood [18] cited as 'very recently' relative to the 2016 submission date.
paper_title: On the number of cliques in graphs with a forbidden minor

title: Characterization of Social Graphs
statement_text: Characterize social graphs, i.e., graphs $G$ such that contracting any edge of $G$ does not increase the number of cliques of $G$.
context_text: Social graphs are introduced as a key structural tool in the proof of Theorem 1.2: the authors show that any very dense social graph is, apart from a small number of vertices, the complement of a (not necessarily perfect) matching. The paper explicitly notes that a full characterization of social graphs appears to be a challenging problem.
notes: Stated in running prose: 'It appears to be a challenging problem to characterize social graphs.'
paper_title: On the number of cliques in graphs with a forbidden minor
